EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This snowfall event generally began on the 27th under a moist southwest flow which followed on the heels of a storm which vacated the area earlier on the same day. The moist southwest flow aloft preceded a potent Pacific trough which moved over eastern Utah on January 28th.

CZ_NAME: GREAT SALT LAKE DESERT AND MOUNTAINS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving and moist storm system moved through Utah beginning on April 8. Heavy snow fell across much of the higher terrain and a few valley locations. In addition, strong downslope winds developed along the Wasatch Front, causing damage across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gusty north winds developed across the Great Salt Lake Desert and Mountains, with a peak recorded wind gust of 63 mph at both Diddle Knoll and Upper Cedar Mountain. In addition, gusts of 55 to 57 mph were recorded at several sites in the Dugway Proving Ground mesonet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An avalanche occurred in Big Cottonwood Canyon, resulting in one fatality.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This particular avalanche fatality attracted attention because the victim, a 34-year-old male, was employed by the Utah Department of Transportation to forecast and control avalanche conditions. The victim was on duty, doing fieldwork alone on Kessler Peak (in Big Cottonwood Canyon), when he apparently ventured just a foot or two off of the safer snow. This triggered a small avalanche, but one that traveled through many sparse trees and over a couple cliffs. When he did not report in later that day, the search for him began; he was eventually found on top of the snow near the bottom of the avalanche, but he had already passed away from his injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: June 2013 proved to be a very dry and warm month across eastern Utah. These conditions had some impact on agricultural interests, though the spring snow pack run off temporarily aided hydrologic interests. The biggest impact however was a noticeable increase in wildfire activity, with several large fires reported across the eastern part of the state through the month. The two climate stations in this part of Utah recording only a trace of precipitation, both a half of an inch or more below normal. This allowed extreme drought conditions to move back into the southeast corner of Utah by the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate drought conditions remained unchanged across the eastern Uinta mountain range and adjacent foothills in June.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Precipitation was more widespread across eastern Utah during the early winter period, but the greater impact occurred across the northern basins and mountains where extreme drought conditions improved to the severe drought category in many areas. One storm in particular picked up sub-tropical moisture and brought over an inch of rainfall to portions of southeast Utah and spread heavy snow across the eastern mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme to severe drought conditions remained unchanged across the area through the month of January.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A less active weather pattern was in place over eastern Utah through the month of February. Winter weather systems still moved over the region but moisture was limited and only a few of these systems were strong enough to bring precipitation to the valleys. The climate stations in eastern Utah reported monthly precipitation values in a deficit of around a third of an inch. However the precipitation was enough to slightly ease drought conditions across most of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme to drought conditions across the northern portion of the zone and severe conditions in the south improved to severe and moderate respectively by the end of February.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A less active weather pattern was in place over eastern Utah through the month of February. Winter weather systems still moved over the region but moisture was limited and only a few of these systems were strong enough to bring precipitation to the valleys. The climate stations in eastern Utah reported monthly precipitation values in a deficit of around a third of an inch. However the precipitation was enough to slightly ease drought conditions across most of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions improved to the moderate category across southeast Utah by the end of February.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions remained unchanged across most of eastern Utah in March as below normal precipitation continued to impact the region. The eastern Uinta Basin received close to normal precipitation at 0.68 inches, but most of this fell with one storm early in the month. Areas of southern Utah didn't fare as well with a deficit of liquid precipitation close to 0.75 of an inch near Moab. As a result severe to moderate drought conditions remained in place through the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions remained unchanged across the eastern Uinta Basin through the month.

The far southeast portion of the state was not as lucky where drought conditions worsened slightly.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate drought conditions remained unchanged across the eastern Uinta Mountains through May.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Precipitation was more widespread across eastern Utah during the early winter period, but the greater impact occurred across the northern basins and mountains where extreme drought conditions improved to the severe drought category in many areas. One storm in particular picked up sub-tropical moisture and brought over an inch of rainfall to portions of southeast Utah and spread heavy snow across the eastern mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme to severe drought conditions remained unchanged across the northeast Utah mountains through the month of January.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered high-based thunderstorms developed across northern Utah a few days in a row during the second week of June. Some of these storms produced strong, gusty winds across the Great Salt Lake Desert and Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak recorded wind gusts from a thunderstorm near Wendover included 63 mph at Wendover Peak and 61 mph at the Wendover Point of Entry.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually strong, cold, and moist upper trough for April resulted in heavy snowfall and blizzard conditions in most mountain areas of eastern Utah, as well as extensive blowing snow in the eastern Uintah Basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 12 to 13 inches were measured across the area. Wind gusts of 20 to 40 mph produced areas of blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually strong, cold, and moist upper trough for April resulted in heavy snowfall and blizzard conditions in most mountain areas of eastern Utah, as well as extensive blowing snow in the eastern Uintah Basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall and wind gusts of 35 to 50 mph produced widespread blowing and drifting snow, along with localized blizzard conditions. Visibilities generally dropped down to about a half mile. Actual snowfall amounts were estimated to be less than an inch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The April storm track did not significantly impact most of eastern Utah, with precipitation in most locations near to slightly below normal for the month. As a result, drought conditions generally remained the same, though some improvement was experienced across the Uinta Mountains, while conditions worsened a little in the four corners region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Uinta Mountains benefited from an early spring storm which improved drought conditions from severe to moderate by the end of the month.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme drought conditions improved to the severe drought category across the eastern Uinta Basin during January, except for the far eastern portion of the basin which remained in extreme drought conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually strong, cold, and moist upper trough for April resulted in heavy snowfall and blizzard conditions in most mountain areas of eastern Utah, as well as extensive blowing snow in the eastern Uintah Basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snowfall and strong winds up to 50 mph produced widespread blizzard conditions with snow drifts up to 4 feet deep. Snowfall measurements in protected areas ranged from 12 to 19 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Significant strides were made during a very wet September against the lingering drought in eastern Utah. This was due to a lingering monsoon which brought abundant rainfall to most of the climate reporting sites across the region. Many locations received in excess of an inch of water above that which normally occurs in September.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme to severe drought conditions improved significantly across the canyon land region of southeast Utah as moderate to abnormally dry conditions moved in by late September.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms continued across Utah for the beginning of August. These storms produced flash flooding across primarily central Utah, and one storm produced a strong microburst in the Salt Lake Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was reported along Sulphur Creek in Capitol Reef National Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms continued across Utah for the beginning of August. These storms produced flash flooding across primarily central Utah, and one storm produced a strong microburst in the Salt Lake Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was reported along Capitol Gorge in Capitol Reef National Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms formed on August 12, producing flash flooding in Capitol Reef National Park and wind damage in northern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding occurred in Capitol Reef National Park, with rangers reporting more than a foot of water running down the Grand Wash. The flooding also resulted in the closing of the Scenic Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoon moisture returned to Utah on August 17, helping to produce heavy thunderstorms across the state, which resulted in several flash floods.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread flash flooding was reported along many normally dry washes and canyons, including Spooky Gulch, 25 Mile Wash, the Egypt 3 slot canyon, and several others.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoon moisture returned to Utah on August 17, helping to produce heavy thunderstorms across the state, which resulted in several flash floods.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was reported in the Johnson Canyon area, as well as Lick Wash. In Lick Wash, hikers scrambled out of the wash for an hour and a half to get away from the flood waters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The deep monsoonal moisture remained over Utah through the end of August, resulting in more flash flooding and severe wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Wendover AWOS recorded a peak wind gust of 79 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture remained in place over Utah, resulting in continued flash flooding in central and southern Utah, particularly at burn scars. In northern Utah, several thunderstorms became severe, producing gusty winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down several trees on Fairfield Road in Layton.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant flash flooding was reported across much of the western side of Zion National Park, including in the Subway area, in Kolob Terrace, and along Taylor Creek in the Kolob area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Small flash floods were reported in Capitol Reef National Park, but the Fremont River rose significantly when all of these washes combined into the main river as it flowed toward Caineville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large and cold storm system moved through Utah in the beginning of December, bringing heavy snowfall to most of the state. Only the far northern parts of Utah were spared from significant snow accumulation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Trial Lake SNOTEL received 1.30 inches of liquid equivalent precipitation, or approximately 20 inches of new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The same storm system that brought strong winds to northern Utah also brought heavy snowfall to the southern half of the state. This storm was unusually cold and snowy for southern Utah, particularly this early in the season.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large and cold storm system moved through Utah in the beginning of December, bringing heavy snowfall to most of the state. Only the far northern parts of Utah were spared from significant snow accumulation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cedar City received a storm total of 12 inches of snow. Snow accumulation impaired travel on area roads, and there were 22 car accidents on Interstate 15 in Iron County, with no injuries reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist Pacific storm system moved into Utah, with precipitation beginning on December 19. Cold air trapped in the valleys of northern Utah helped produce some freezing rain, before switching to all snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain was less significant in the Cache Valley than other parts of the state, but snowfall was quite heavy on the morning of December 19. Storm total snowfall included 8 inches in Providence and 7 inches in Logan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist Pacific storm system moved into Utah, with precipitation beginning on December 19. Cold air trapped in the valleys of northern Utah helped produce some freezing rain, before switching to all snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snowfall from the Wasatch mountain valleys included 8 inches near Kamas and 6 inches of snow in Park City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold and slow-moving storm system moved through Utah on March 8 and 9. The heaviest precipitation with this storm fell over the southern half of Utah, with heavy snow observed in both mountain and valley locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fillmore received 8 inches of snow for the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold storm system moved into northern Utah on March 22, setting up ideal conditions for lake effect snow overnight and into the 23rd. The area with the most significant snow accumulation from this lake effect band was the Salt Lake Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lake effect snow band formed on the evening of March 22, and persisted through the morning of March 23. Storm totals across the Salt Lake Valley included 8 inches of snow in West Jordan and 6 inches of snow in Draper.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold and slow-moving storm system moved through Utah on March 8 and 9. The heaviest precipitation with this storm fell over the southern half of Utah, with heavy snow observed in both mountain and valley locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Kanab received a storm total of 7 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold and slow-moving storm system moved through Utah on March 8 and 9. The heaviest precipitation with this storm fell over the southern half of Utah, with heavy snow observed in both mountain and valley locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Richfield received 10 inches of snow for the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold and slow-moving storm system moved through Utah on March 8 and 9. The heaviest precipitation with this storm fell over the southern half of Utah, with heavy snow observed in both mountain and valley locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Pine Creek SNOTEL received 2.40 inches of liquid equivalent precipitation, or approximately 26 inches of new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold and slow-moving storm system moved through Utah on March 8 and 9. The heaviest precipitation with this storm fell over the southern half of Utah, with heavy snow observed in both mountain and valley locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Webster Flat SNOTEL received 2.40 inches of liquid equivalent precipitation, or approximately 30 inches of new snow. In addition, the SR-12 at Boulder Summit sensor recorded a maximum wind gust of 80 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front entered Utah on the morning of February 23, producing gusty winds and heavy snow, primarily across the northern half of the state. Snow showers continued through the night and into the morning of the 24th, especially east and south of the Great Salt Lake.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Logan received 8 inches of new snow from the storm, most of which fell within the first few hours of the cold frontal passage.

CZ_NAME: GREAT SALT LAKE DESERT AND MOUNTAINS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front entered Utah on the morning of February 23, producing gusty winds and heavy snow, primarily across the northern half of the state. Snow showers continued through the night and into the morning of the 24th, especially east and south of the Great Salt Lake.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Travel was greatly impaired across the Great Salt Lake Desert, particularly along Interstate 80, with very low visibilities and accumulating snow on roadways for a few hours. Winds were strong, creating widespread blowing and drifting snow, with peak recorded wind gusts of 69 mph at the V-Grid sensor in the Dugway Proving Ground mesonet, 63 mph at the Wendover AWOS, and several other gusts in the 50 mph range along Interstate 80.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front entered Utah on the morning of February 23, producing gusty winds and heavy snow, primarily across the northern half of the state. Snow showers continued through the night and into the morning of the 24th, especially east and south of the Great Salt Lake.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Liberty received a storm total of 14 inches new snow. In addition, gusty winds up to 40 mph with the cold frontal passage led to impaired travel, with widespread blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front entered Utah on the morning of February 23, producing gusty winds and heavy snow, primarily across the northern half of the state. Snow showers continued through the night and into the morning of the 24th, especially east and south of the Great Salt Lake.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Suncrest received a storm total of 10 inches of snow, while Pleasant Grove received 7 inches of new snow. In addition, travel was impaired along the southern Wasatch Front, with approximately 40 automobile accidents reported across the area. Visibilities were low for several hours, and wind gusts up to 40 mph caused blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front entered Utah on the morning of February 23, producing gusty winds and heavy snow, primarily across the northern half of the state. Snow showers continued through the night and into the morning of the 24th, especially east and south of the Great Salt Lake.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm totals across the Salt Lake Valley included 10 inches of new snow in Sandy, 9 inches in Cottonwood Heights, and 8 inches of snow in Upper Millcreek. In addition, travel was impaired along the Salt Lake and Tooele Valleys; 129 crashes were reported in Salt Lake County alone, with 12 of those resulting in injuries. These adverse conditions included very low visibilities for several hours, as well as strong wind gusts that created widespread blowing and drifting snow. Peak recorded wind gusts included 66 mph at the Lake Point at Interstate 80 sensor, 60 mph at Vernon Hill, and 58 mph at Burmester.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two boys were killed in an avalanche in the Uinta Mountains during a snowmobile trip with their family.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A family was out for a day of snowmobiling in the Uinta Mountains, when they decided to take a break for lunch in a wide open basin on the West Fork of the Duchesne River. The kids walked to a edge of a ridge to see over the side when the ledge gave way, catching three of the children in an avalanche, and burying two of them. The two boys were eventually dug out of the snow and carried by helicopter to a local hospital, but they passed away that evening due to their injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered high-based thunderstorms formed across northern Utah on May 15, primarily bringing gusty microburst winds to the area. The strongest winds with these thunderstorms were recorded across the Great Salt Lake Desert.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The West Salt Flats sensor in the Dugway Proving Ground mesonet recorded a peak wind gust of 61 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During an active evening of showers of thunderstorms, damage was reported from two different lightning strikes near Salt Lake City.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck the top gable of a house in West Jordan; the bolt then traveled about 10 feet and burned part of a satellite dish on the roof of the house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During an active evening of showers of thunderstorms, damage was reported from two different lightning strikes near Salt Lake City.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A plane belonging to the Utah Air National Guard (a KC-135, which is a large plane that provides in-flight refueling for other aircraft) took off from Salt Lake City International Airport on the evening of May 7, and was struck by lightning about 10 minutes after takeoff. The lightning damaged a communications antenna that stretches from the nose to the tail of the aircraft. In addition to knocking out communications, the antenna became loose and hit against the plane repeatedly, damaging the skin of the aircraft. No one was injured in the incident, and the crew was able to use alternate communications to help them return to the airport and land.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Gusty easterly canyon winds developed along the northern Wasatch Front on the night of May 4, peaking just before midnight before beginning to drop off.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A sensor in Farmington, Utah recorded a peak wind gust of 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A primarily dry but windy storm system moved through Utah on May 22 and 23, with isolated sites in northern Utah recording severe winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Chepeta RAWS recorded several hours of strong wind gusts, with a peak gust of 84 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Antelope Island State Park sensor recorded a maximum wind gust of 68 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A widespread and long-lasting heatwave developed across the western United States at the end of June and continued into the first week of July. Near the end of this heatwave, a few heat-related deaths occurred in southern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hot temperatures contributed to the deaths of three hikers in southern Utah. On July 3, an elderly couple from California received permits to hike The Wave, a popular destination in far southern Utah. Both victims were found on the morning of July 4, and it is believed that they died during the afternoon of the 3rd, when temperatures were close to 100 degrees.||The third victim, a 35-year-old female from Denver, passed away on the 4th of July. She was hiking with a friend near Spooky Gulch in the Grand Staircase-Escalante National Monument. The two women became lost for a couple hours, before other hikers found them and helped them back toward the trailhead. The victim was struggling in the heat and ran out of water near the trailhead, so her friend ran to find help. When she returned, the victim had no pulse, and all efforts to revive her were unsuccessful.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The daily potential for flash flooding was relentless through the middle of July, with multiple flash floods during this period resulting in property damage. In addition, temperatures were hot across southern Utah, with another heat related death occurring near the Arizona border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hot temperatures returned to southern Utah in late July, and another heat-related death occurred at The Wave, a popular hike near the Utah/Arizona border. A 27-year-old Arizona woman was hiking The Wave with her husband on July 22, when they got lost and spent several hours trying to find their way back to their vehicle. Around 2 p.m., the woman collapsed, and her husband left to get help. When help arrived, she was already in cardiac arrest, and she passed away that afternoon, as emergency responders were unable to revive her.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding occurred in Zion National Park, with the largest reported flash floods in Mystery Canyon and the Narrows. A group of people became stranded in Mystery Canyon during the flood, and had to be rescued by park rangers.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a week where only isolated flash flooding impacted the state of Utah, a very moist air mass moved over the area once again. Flash flooding was reported in many locations across the state, including nearly-daily debris flows coming off the Seeley Fire burn scar.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A stronger secondary wave of flash flooding occurred along Huntington Creek, with a rise of five feet along the creek observed at the PacifiCorp Huntington Plant. This time, the heavy rain also resulted in a debris flow at the Seeley Fire burn scar, resulting in State Route 31 being covered in sand and silt between mileposts 24 and 34. This also resulted in some debris flowing in the river near Huntington Plant, including a couple of large logs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong, but dry, cold front moved through Utah on June 13, producing strong gusty winds in multiple locations, primarily in the northern half of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak recorded wind gusts across the southern Wasatch Front included 72 mph at Flight Park South, 68 mph at Saratoga Springs, 63 mph at the U.S. 6 at Eureka sensor, and 59 mph at Lehi.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough along the west coast of California pushed the subtropical high far enough eastward to allow a good push of monsoonal moisture back into the Four Corners region. The increase in thunderstorm activity seen on the 23rd led to heavy rainfall, flash flooding and isolated reports of strong thunderstorm winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A dirt road in the northwest portion of Arches National Park was closed due to mud and water approximately 1 foot deep which flowed over the roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoonal moisture moved into Utah beginning on July 3, allowing for widespread showers and thunderstorms across the state. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered high-based thunderstorms developed across northern Utah a few days in a row during the second week of June. Some of these storms produced strong, gusty winds across the Great Salt Lake Desert and Mountains.

In addition, multiple large trees were knocked down in West Jordan, South Jordan, and Taylorsville, including a few that fell on houses and caused damage to roofs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall resulted in flash flooding within a section of Arches National Park.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff down the Winter Camp Wash from late day thunderstorms north of Arches National Park on August 24th reached a road near Delicate Arch in Arches National Park during the early morning hours of the 25th. Water over a foot deep flowed across a section of the road.  After the flash flood waters receded, the road to the Delicate Arch viewpoint trail head remained closed for several hours during debris removal activities.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A southwest flow around the subtropical high centered over the plains continued to supply subtropical moisture for heavy rain producing thunderstorms over Western Colorado. Rainfall from some of these storms resulted in localized flash flooding on August 25th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall through the day, with 1.85 inches recorded between 2 pm and 3 pm, produced flash flooding from the town of Bluff to Sand Island and along Highway 191. Sand bagging was needed in town to protect structures.  Minor damage was reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain during the overnight hours caused flash flooding in Little Cottonwood Canyon, resulting in rockslides and mudslides onto the main road along the canyon. The flood deposited debris onto the road, including trees, branches, and rocks. This debris was spread over a large area of the road, from 4 miles down the canyon from Snowbird Ski and Summer Resort, all the way down to the bottom of the canyon. Twelve hours of cleanup was necessary before the road was reopened to automobile traffic. The road was damaged in some locations, particularly along the shoulder, but the integrity of the main roadway was not compromised. The Utah Department of Transportation estimated that the cost of the road repairs would be approximately $80,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The threat of severe thunderstorms returned for a couple of days in the middle of the month, with both strong, gusty winds and large hail being reported across Utah. In addition, lightning caused multiple fires in northern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A fire started at a Salt Lake City green waste storage site, with lightning as the suspected cause of the fire. The fire was contained to a 200-by-300-foot pit where mulch was stored, and firefighters were able to extinguish the blaze before it spread to any adjacent buildings.

Boulders could be heard rolling in the strong current. Dirt roads in the area were impassable due to the deep and fast flowing water that inundated them.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rainfall converged on the Monticello area during the late morning hours. Localized urban flooding was noted as drainage systems struggled to handle the runoff.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall of nearly three quarters of an inch was reported in the southwest part of Monticello which led to localized street flooding. Heavier amounts of precipitation fell in other portions of the city which also led to flooding of basements.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The final week of July saw even more flash flooding across Utah. The afternoons of July 26 and 27 were especially destructive, with record-breaking rainfall and widespread flooding. An isolated severe thunderstorm was also recorded during this period of time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding occurred along the Paria River, with the gauge along the Paria River at Kanab rising from 0 cfs to 2648 cfs in two hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The threat of severe thunderstorms returned for a couple of days in the middle of the month, with both strong, gusty winds and large hail being reported across Utah. In addition, lightning caused multiple fires in northern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a cluster of oil storage tanks just outside Altamont, starting a fire at the facility. At least one of the tanks exploded, sending 50-foot-tall flames into the air, and creating a loud boom that woke people in Altamont. One tank was launched outside the containment berm around the well location, and two other storage tanks were engulfed in flames before firefighters reached the site. Once crews shut down the gas line that runs near the well, firefighters were able to put out the fire in less than two hours, and no injuries occurred due to the fire or explosion.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front entered Utah on the morning of February 23, producing gusty winds and heavy snow, primarily across the northern half of the state. Snow showers continued through the night and into the morning of the 24th, especially east and south of the Great Salt Lake.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: South Ogden received 10 inches of new snow for the storm. In addition, travel was impaired along the northern Wasatch Front, with approximately 60 car accidents reported. Four of these accidents resulted in injuries, including a highway patrol trooper who was hit while providing traffic control for a crash on Interstate 15 in Ogden. Visibilities were very low for several hours, and wind gusts of up to 46 mph created widespread blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rare freezing rain event occurred in Utah on January 24. Very cold air was trapped in valley locations due to a persistent inversion; when a weather disturbance brought precipitation to the area, and the system was too weak to break the inversion, freezing rain occurred at the surface, bringing widespread travel difficulties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Of all the areas across Utah, the Salt Lake Valley was perhaps hit the hardest by the freezing rain event. Icy roads caused many automobile accidents, including 128 collisions, 25 accidents resulting in injuries, and 8 slide-offs in Salt Lake County. Two highway patrol officers were struck by out of control cars, with both incidents occurring on Interstate 15. Hundreds of people were injured due to slipping and falling on the ice, with over 100 patients treated for ice-related injuries at Intermountain Medical Center in Murray alone. The freezing rain also had major impacts at the Salt Lake City International Airport, with many flights delayed due to the icy runways. The entire airport was closed for three hours in the late morning and early afternoon, after a Frontier Airlines plane slid down the taxiway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rare freezing rain event occurred in Utah on January 24. Very cold air was trapped in valley locations due to a persistent inversion; when a weather disturbance brought precipitation to the area, and the system was too weak to break the inversion, freezing rain occurred at the surface, bringing widespread travel difficulties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The freezing rain was less significant in the Utah Valley than further northward, but still caused travel difficulties. The slick roads were blamed for 90 accidents in the area, two of which caused injuries. In addition, a highway patrol trooper was injured when an out-of-control car struck a cruiser, which was parked while the officer was assisting another vehicle that had slid off the road in Springville. Injuries were also common from people slipping on the ice while walking. Utah Valley Regional Medical Center in Provo reported 70 ice-related injuries, including one death attributed to the icy conditions.

CZ_NAME: GREAT SALT LAKE DESERT AND MOUNTAINS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front associated with a fast-moving weather disturbance brought gusty winds to northern Utah, with the strongest winds in the Great Salt Lake Desert.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak recorded wind gusts across the Great Salt Lake Desert included 74 mph at Wendover Peak, 66 mph at Diddle Knoll, and 60 mph at the Interstate 80 at Milepost 29 sensor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed across Utah at the end of the month, as a weakening low pressure system moved across the Great Basin. Due to dry air in the lower levels, many of these showers produced gusty winds, with an isolated storm even reaching severe thunderstorm status.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The SR-12 at Boulder Summit sensor recorded a peak wind gust of 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An avalanche was triggered in the area near 12 Mile Canyon, in Sevier County, Utah. The avalanche caught and killed one snowmobiler.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A group of snowmobilers were recreating on the southern end of the Manti Skyline, just east out of 12 Mile Canyon, in Sevier County. For part of the afternoon, most of the group members were riding independently; however, around 4:00 pm, the group realized one of the members was missing. They had neither heard nor seen the avalanche, but they soon found the path the avalanche had taken, and correctly assumed that the missing group member had been buried under the snow. The group tried to dig out the missing person, a 32-year-old male, and eventually went for help from the local authorities. By the time they were able to find him, nearly five hours later, he had already passed away.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving and moist storm system moved through Utah beginning on April 8. Heavy snow fell across much of the higher terrain and a few valley locations. In addition, strong downslope winds developed along the Wasatch Front, causing damage across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Easterly downslope winds impacted the Salt Lake Valley, though not as severely as in the northern Wasatch Front. Olympus Cove recorded a peak wind gust of 63 mph, while multiple other sensors across the area recorded wind gusts of 55 mph. Several large trees were knocked over across the valley; some of these trees fell onto vehicles, including two vehicles damaged in Olympus Cove and two vehicles damaged in the Sugarhouse area. Power lines were knocked down in multiple locations, most notably in South Salt Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving and moist storm system moved through Utah beginning on April 8. Heavy snow fell across much of the higher terrain and a few valley locations. In addition, strong downslope winds developed along the Wasatch Front, causing damage across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Easterly downslope winds most severely impacted the northern Wasatch Front. Peak recorded wind gusts included 75 mph at Centerville and 65 mph at Farmington. Interstate 15 was closed to high profile vehicles for several hours due to the strong winds. The worst individual incident was a light pole that was blown over at a car dealership in North Salt Lake; the pole landed on several vehicles, causing significant damage to the bed of one pickup truck and some damage to a second truck. Sporadic power outages were reported due to the wind, including multiple transformers that blew out in Weber Canyon. Public infrastructure escaped relatively unscathed, though a few street signs were knocked over or bent. Damage to private property included many downed trees, including a few trees that fell on automobiles, wooden and vinyl fences that were destroyed, shingles that were removed from roofs, and trampolines that went flying out of several backyards. Blowing debris was common, particularly in neighborhoods that had scheduled trash days, but no injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Recent snowfall increased moisture in the lower atmosphere which became trapped under high pressure.  This resulted in areas of dense fog.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dense fog occurred across portions of Grand County, including the Canyonlands Aiport where visibilities dropped down to a quarter mile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture remained trapped under an inversion which allowed dense fog to form across portions of southeast Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dense fog occurred in the town of Blanding as visibilities dropped down to a quarter mile or less  The fog satellite product also showed widespread fog in the Blanding area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture became trapped under a ridge of high pressure which caused dense fog to form.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dense fog occurred across eastern Grand County with visibilities down to a quarter mile or less.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low level moisture remained trapped under an area of high pressure which caused dense fog to form.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dense fog occurred across portions of the Uintah Basin as visibilities dropped down to a quarter mile or less.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific trough brushed northeast Utah and produced significant early season snowfall in the eastern Uinta Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 2 to 5 inches were measured across the area, including 4 inches on sections of Highway 44.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist and unseasonably cold upper low moved slowly across the region and produced heavy amounts of early season snowfall in the mountains of northeast Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 5 to 13 inches were measured across the area.  Numerous vehicle accidents occurred due to the slick roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist and unseasonably cold upper low moved slowly across the region and produced heavy amounts of early season snowfall in the mountains of northeast Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 3 to 7 inches were measured across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The persistent flow of monsoonal moisture resulted in strong thunderstorms which resulted in large hail and heavy rainfall in portions of southeast Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of 2.22 inches fell within a 6 hour period at Cedar Point.  About 1.50 inches of that rainfall occurred within 45 minutes beginning around 12:30 MST in the afternoon.  Minor flooding was reported in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The persistent flow of monsoonal moisture resulted in strong thunderstorms which resulted in large hail and heavy rainfall in portions of southeast Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball sized hail broke windows and windshields, among other damage, along Red Mesa Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The persistent flow of monsoonal moisture resulted in strong thunderstorms which resulted in large hail and heavy rainfall in portions of southeast Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winter Camp, Salt Wash, and Salt Valley washes all flash flooded with water running across the road to Delicate Arch. The road was closed as water continued to run across it for several hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The persistent flow of monsoonal moisture resulted in strong thunderstorms which resulted in large hail and heavy rainfall in portions of southeast Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail fell along Highway 191 about seven miles north of Blanding as reported by the sheriff's office.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The persistent flow of monsoonal moisture resulted in strong thunderstorms which resulted in large hail and heavy rainfall in portions of southeast Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rain in the Monticello area created flash flooding, with flowing water at least 6 inches deep across country roadways just south of Monticello.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large, moist storm system moved through the region in mid-April. One of the main features with this storm was a cold frontal boundary that stalled across Utah for more than a day, bringing heavy snowfall to the valleys of southwest and northeast Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fillmore received 10 inches of new snow for the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large, moist storm system moved through the region in mid-April. One of the main features with this storm was a cold frontal boundary that stalled across Utah for more than a day, bringing heavy snowfall to the valleys of southwest and northeast Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cedar City received a storm total of 9 inches new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large, moist storm system moved through the region in mid-April. One of the main features with this storm was a cold frontal boundary that stalled across Utah for more than a day, bringing heavy snowfall to the valleys of southwest and northeast Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Altamont, Utah received the heaviest recorded snowfall from the event, as the cold frontal boundary set up over the Uinta Basin for an extended period of time. For the storm, Altamont received a total of 19 inches new snow.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Lightning Ridge SNOTEL received 2.20 inches of liquid equivalent precipitation, or approximately 25 inches of new snow for the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large and cold storm system moved through Utah in the beginning of December, bringing heavy snowfall to most of the state. Only the far northern parts of Utah were spared from significant snow accumulation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snowfall included 13 inches at Elk Ridge, 12 inches in Spanish Fork, and 11 inches in Springville and Provo. Utah County reported 75 car accidents, with seven injuries.

CZ_NAME: UTAHS DIXIE AND ZION NATIONAL PARK
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large and cold storm system moved through Utah in the beginning of December, bringing heavy snowfall to most of the state. Only the far northern parts of Utah were spared from significant snow accumulation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Springdale received 3 inches of snow, while St. George received 2 inches of snow. The rare snowfall caused at least 30 car accidents in Washington County, with one reported injury. A few of these automobile accidents caused  property damage, including a fire hydrant that was hit, and a tree branch that fell onto a vehicle.

CZ_NAME: UTAHS DIXIE AND ZION NATIONAL PARK
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold and large winter storm moved through Utah beginning December 7, once again impacting both northern and southern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Unusually large amounts of snow fell in Utah's Dixie, with storm total snowfall of 14 inches at Zion National Park, 13 inches in Springdale, 8 inches in Ivins, 7 inches in La Verkin, and 6 inches in both St. George and Hurricane. The snow caused significant travel difficulties in the area, especially considering many cities do not own any snowplows. Interstate 15 was closed due to snowfall in the Virgin River Gorge just southwest of St. George, stranding about 300 people on the highway overnight in Arizona. In addition, a semitrailer traveling northbound on Interstate 15 jackknifed in snowy conditions, closing two lanes of the highway for a couple hours in the Black Ridge area of Washington County. Also, the St. George airport was closed for most of the day on December 7, as the long period of snowfall overwhelmed its single snow plow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold and large winter storm moved through Utah beginning December 7, once again impacting both northern and southern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mammoth-Cottonwood SNOTEL received a storm total of 0.90 inches liquid equivalent precipitation, or approximately 20 inches of new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist Pacific storm system moved into Utah, with precipitation beginning on December 19. Cold air trapped in the valleys of northern Utah helped produce some freezing rain, before switching to all snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm totals from the northern Wasatch Mountains included 19 inches of snow at Snowbasin Resort and 17 inches of new snow at Powder Mountain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following a deep winter storm, very cold temperatures settled into Utah. Some property damage was reported due to these temperatures, primarily across the northern Wasatch Front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lows near or below zero degrees Fahrenheit were common across the northern Wasatch Front, and freezing pipes and sprinkler systems caused flood damage in a couple different buildings. An overhead sprinkler froze at the Farmington courthouse, damaging the clerical area, including computers and copier machines. In Ogden, the Shepherd Union Building at Weber State University experienced flood damage after a portion of the fire sprinkler system froze. The broken pipe caused a section of ceiling to collapse above one of the student lounges, damaging some furniture and parts of the campus store. Other issues caused by cold across the area included school buses being unable to start, and the closure of Highland Junior High School in Ogden when the temperatures froze a boiler coil, disabling the heating system.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough located in the Four Corners area brought slow moving thunderstorms to portions of southeast Utah. Some of these storms also contained heavy rain and led to flash flooding in the southern extent of Canyonlands National Park.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: National Park Service rangers reported flash flooding across Salt Creek Road In Canyonlands National Park.  The flash flood had originated from Horse Canyon due to heavy rainfall. The water also created areas of quicksand which made the road impassable after the flood waters subsided.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The same storm system that brought strong winds to northern Utah also brought heavy snowfall to the southern half of the state. This storm was unusually cold and snowy for southern Utah, particularly this early in the season.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm totals in Utah's southern mountains included 22 inches of snow at Brian Head Resort and 19 inches at Eagle Point.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The same storm system that brought strong winds to northern Utah also brought heavy snowfall to the southern half of the state. This storm was unusually cold and snowy for southern Utah, particularly this early in the season.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Escalante received 18.5 inches of snow over a 48-hour period. Since records began in Escalante in 1901, this was the thirteenth highest snow total over a 3 calendar day period, and the highest value ever recorded earlier in the season than January.

CZ_NAME: GREAT SALT LAKE DESERT AND MOUNTAINS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist Pacific storm system moved into Utah, with precipitation beginning on December 19. Cold air trapped in the valleys of northern Utah helped produce some freezing rain, before switching to all snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snowfall impacted travel across Utah's northwest desert. The Utah Test and Training Range received 3 inches of new snow, with more snowfall likely in the higher terrain of the area, and 30 slide-offs were reported along Interstate 80 in Tooele County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist Pacific storm system moved into Utah, with precipitation beginning on December 19. Cold air trapped in the valleys of northern Utah helped produce some freezing rain, before switching to all snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Salt Lake Valley was perhaps the hardest hit by the combination of freezing rain and heavy snowfall. Most of the freezing rain occurred during the early morning hours on December 19, before switching to all snow through the morning. Storm total snowfall across the valley included 10 inches in Herriman, 8 inches in Sandy, and 7 inches in West Valley City and Draper. ||Salt Lake City International Airport was shut down for a few hours in the morning, and over 200 flights were cancelled due to the slick runways. Travel by automobile was slow and hazardous, with over 450 accidents reported in the Salt Lake Valley, and 24 minor injuries from those accidents. Power outages were common, with over 14,000 customers losing power. Along State Route 201, a utility pole broke, with power lines falling across the roadway. These power lines shut down the road for several hours in both directions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist Pacific storm system moved into Utah, with precipitation beginning on December 19. Cold air trapped in the valleys of northern Utah helped produce some freezing rain, before switching to all snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snowfall included 16 inches at Brighton Resort, 13 inches at Alta Ski Lifts, and a foot of new snow at Snowbird Ski and Summer Resort and Deer Valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist Pacific storm system moved into Utah, with precipitation beginning on December 19. Cold air trapped in the valleys of northern Utah helped produce some freezing rain, before switching to all snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A combination of freezing rain and heavy snowfall caused slick roads in the northern Wasatch Front, with about 40 automobile accidents reported in Davis County. Snowfall was heavy on the morning of December 19, with generally about 7 inches of new snow reported across the area. Locations that reported 7 inches of snowfall included Clearfield, South Ogden, Centerville, Bountiful, and Bountiful Bench.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving upper level low centered near the Four Corners brought heavy rain with scattered thunderstorms to east central Utah. The rainfall produced by these storms lead to flooding in some areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winter Camp wash was flooded during the overnight hours causing the a road in Arches National Park to be closed for a number of hours due to a foot or more of water and debris flowing over the roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rare freezing rain event occurred in Utah on January 24. Very cold air was trapped in valley locations due to a persistent inversion; when a weather disturbance brought precipitation to the area, and the system was too weak to break the inversion, freezing rain occurred at the surface, bringing widespread travel difficulties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain caused hazardous driving conditions along the northern Wasatch Front, with over 50 accidents attributed to the icy roads. Injuries due to slips and falls on the ice were common, with at least 100 injuries reported. Lakeview Hospital in Bountiful reported that the number of patients in the emergency room was the highest single-day total in the history of the hospital.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A woman died due to heat exhaustion while hiking in Capitol Reef National Park.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 56-year-old woman and her husband were hiking the Brimhall Double Bridge Trail in Capitol Reef National Park on August 15, when the woman became ill. The man went to get help, but she had passed away by the time medical personnel arrived. The cause of death was heat exhaustion, with temperatures exceeding 90 degrees. The time listed is approximate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold and powerful storm system moved into Utah beginning on the 10th of January. The heaviest snow in many locations fell on the 11th, before snow showers tapered off on the 12th. Because this storm was so cold, snow ratios were higher in the valley locations of northern Utah than at the higher elevations. In some cases, this resulted in two to three times as much snow in the valleys than in nearby adjacent mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Pine Creek SNOTEL received 1.50 inches of liquid equivalent precipitation, or approximately 24 inches of new snow. In addition, winds were strong with the passage of the initial cold front, with the Signal Peak RAWS recording a maximum wind gust of 80 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dense fog formed across the area just before sunrise and persisted into the early morning hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extensive fog was reported around the area, and especially in the late night hours to mid morning hours when visibilities dropped to a quarter mile or less.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold and powerful storm system moved into Utah beginning on the 10th of January. The heaviest snow in many locations fell on the 11th, before snow showers tapered off on the 12th. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm totals across the Salt Lake Valley included 34 inches of snow at Upper Millcreek, 20 inches at Olympus Hills and the Upper Avenues, 19 inches in Holladay, 14 inches in South Salt Lake, and 10 inches of new snow at Salt Lake City International Airport. This snow seriously impaired travel across the area, with Salt Lake County reporting the most accidents in the entire state, with a total of 108 property damage incidents and 128 slide-offs, as well as 7 reported injuries. Strong winds were also observed ahead of and with the initial cold front on January 10, including a 61 mph wind gust at the Great Salt Lake Marina, and 60 mph wind gusts at Olympus Hills and Olympus Cove. These winds knocked over and destroyed the sign for a KFC restaurant in Sandy.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold and powerful storm system moved into Utah beginning on the 10th of January. In some cases, this resulted in two to three times as much snow in the valleys than in nearby adjacent mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snowfall in the northern Wasatch Front included 34 inches at Bountiful Bench, 27 inches in Centerville, 21 inches at Woods Cross, 19 inches in South Ogden, and 18 inches of new snow in several locations, including Farmington, Clearfield, Hill Air Force Base, and North Salt Lake. This snowfall greatly impacted transportation in the area, with about 100 reported accidents. Many schools cancelled classes, a rare occurrence in the area, including the entire Davis School District.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved into northern Utah on January 27, bringing additional snow to the area, and finally breaking the long-lasting valley inversions. The heaviest snow occurred along the cold front on the 27th, with showers lingering on the 28th, and eventually ending on the 29th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm totals from the Wasatch mountain valleys included 9 inches of new snow at Jeremy Ranch and 8 inches of snow at Liberty.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe to extreme drought conditions were the norm across eastern Utah through the month of July.  Agriculture, hydrologic and fire concerns were all heightened across the region. However, by the end of the month the Southwest Monsoon was beginning to show some of promise of relief. Thunderstorms, some with heavy rain and flooding, became more widespread across the southern and central portion of the area by the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions remained unchanged across the area through July.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple of weather disturbances moved through Utah on January 29 and 30, bringing more snow to northern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm totals from the northern Wasatch Front included 10 inches of snow at Northeast Ogden Bench, 9 inches at North Ogden, and 8 inches of new snow at South Ogden.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple of weather disturbances moved through Utah on January 29 and 30, bringing more snow to northern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: For the two-day storm, Alpine received a storm total of 23 inches of snow, while Draper received 18 inches of new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple of weather disturbances moved through Utah on January 29 and 30, bringing more snow to northern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snowfall included 36 inches at Snowbird Ski and Summer Resort and 30 inches of snow at Alta Ski Lifts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple of weather disturbances moved through Utah on January 29 and 30, bringing more snow to northern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Liberty received a storm total of 32 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold and powerful storm system moved into Utah beginning on the 10th of January. The heaviest snow in many locations fell on the 11th, before snow showers tapered off on the 12th. Because this storm was so cold, snow ratios were higher in the valley locations of northern Utah than at the higher elevations. In some cases, this resulted in two to three times as much snow in the valleys than in nearby adjacent mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Kimberly Mine SNOTEL received 1.10 inches of liquid equivalent precipitation, or approximately 17 inches of new snow, with most of this snowfall occurring in the first six hours of the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe to extreme drought conditions were the norm across eastern Utah through the month of July.  Agriculture, hydrologic and fire concerns were all heightened across the region. However, by the end of the month the Southwest Monsoon was beginning to show some of promise of relief. Thunderstorms, some with heavy rain and flooding, became more widespread across the southern and central portion of the area by the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe to moderate drought conditions remained unchanged across the eastern Uintah Basin through July.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Frigid air spread into southeast Utah in the wake of a passing cold front essentially resulted in ending the growing season for much of southeast Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minimum temperatures dropped to 28 to 32 degrees Fahrenheit across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Frigid air spread into southeast Utah in the wake of a passing cold front essentially resulted in ending the growing season for much of southeast Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minimum temperatures dropped to 28 to 32 degrees Fahrenheit across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large and cold storm system moved through Utah in the beginning of December, bringing heavy snowfall to most of the state. Only the far northern parts of Utah were spared from significant snow accumulation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snowfall included 18 inches at both Deer Valley and Snowbird Ski and Summer Resort, 17 inches at both Alta Ski Lifts and Brighton Resort, and 16 inches at the summit of Park City Mountain Resort.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large and cold storm system moved through Utah in the beginning of December, bringing heavy snowfall to most of the state. Only the far northern parts of Utah were spared from significant snow accumulation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snowfall across the Salt Lake and Tooele Valleys included 11.5 inches in Cottonwood Heights, 10 inches in Millcreek and Sandy, and 9 inches in Stansbury Park. The biggest impact to travel was on Tuesday, December 3, with more than 200 car accidents reported across Salt Lake County, including 20 reported injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large and cold storm system moved through Utah in the beginning of December, bringing heavy snowfall to most of the state. Only the far northern parts of Utah were spared from significant snow accumulation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm total of 10 inches new snow fell across Bountiful, Bountiful Bench, and Centerville. The accumulating snow caused hazardous road conditions, with 62 accidents reported in Davis County on December 3. Davis County proved to be the northern edge of significant impacts with this storm, as the valleys of Weber County just to the north generally recorded 2 inches of snow or less.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold and large winter storm moved through Utah beginning December 7, once again impacting both northern and southern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowbasin Resort received 18 inches of new snow. In addition, the Farmington Lower SNOTEL received 0.90 inches of liquid equivalent precipitation, or approximately 20 inches of new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold and large winter storm moved through Utah beginning December 7, once again impacting both northern and southern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snowfall in the Salt Lake Valley included 12 inches in Magna, 8.5 inches in  Millcreek, and 6 inches in West Valley City, South Salt Lake, and Sandy. Most of the snowfall in Magna occurred on the morning of December 8, as a lake effect snow band set up over the city for an extended period of time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold and moist Pacific storm moved into Utah on the morning of November 16, bringing a period of heavy snow to the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The storm brought heavy snow to the Cottonwood Canyons, with Brighton Resort receiving 17 inches of new snow and Alta Ski Lifts recording 15 inches of new snow. Both of these resorts received most of this snow in a 12-hour period during the daytime hours of November 16.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system moving through southern Utah created an easterly surface gradient in northern Utah, producing gusty downslope winds along portions of the Wasatch Front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Downslope winds were strongest in the northern Wasatch Front, with a peak recorded wind gust of 85 mph at a UDOT shed in Centerville. Other sensors in Centerville and Farmington also recorded 80 mph wind gusts. The wind knocked out power to more than 10,000 residents across the area. Included in these power outages were some traffic signal lights and street lights that were out; these outages, combined with the winds and blowing debris, were blamed for an auto-pedestrian accident in Ogden, which resulted in one injury and one fatality. ||Damage was less severe than other downslope windstorms in recent years, but was  still widespread. Several street signs were bent or knocked over, including four exit signs along Interstate 15. Numerous large trees were uprooted, and damage to fences and roofs was common. An RV in North Ogden was knocked over, and semitrailer traffic was stopped for a period of time along Interstate 15 and Legacy Highway. Other items that sustained damage or became airborne included trampolines, children's playground equipment, and garage doors.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system moving through southern Utah created an easterly surface gradient in northern Utah, producing gusty downslope winds along portions of the Wasatch Front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds in the Salt Lake Valley were considerably weaker than further north, with a peak recorded wind gust of 62 mph at The University of Utah. Damage was also considerably less, with relatively minor damage reported to trees, fences, and playground equipment.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The moist airmass that was over Utah in late August continued through the first few days of September, helping produce several flash floods across the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was reported along the Grand Wash, Sulphur Creek, and the Scenic Drive in Capitol Reef National Park.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Horse Hollow RAWS recorded a peak wind gust of 65 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a brief break from showers and thunderstorms, significant moisture and instability returned to Utah, with flash flooding, large hail, and even a tornado reported across the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large flash flood was reported on Wahweap Creek and the Paria River, as well as smaller drainages in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms continued across Utah for the beginning of August. These storms produced flash flooding across primarily central Utah, and one storm produced a strong microburst in the Salt Lake Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A microburst occurred near the intersection of Utah State Route 111 and New Bingham Highway in West Jordan, knocking over or snapping a series of power poles. Rocky Mountain Power reported that 37 large transmission poles and 4 smaller distribution poles were damaged and had to be replaced. This temporarily knocked out power to about 3,000 customers, as well as closing State Route 111 for several hours. The downed power lines also dragged a couple of metal poles supporting traffic signals to the ground. Despite the destruction, two sensors very close to the intersection (the SR-85 at Dannon Way sensor and the Trans-Jordan Landfill sensor) only recorded peak wind gusts of 45 mph and 42 mph respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific storm system, combined with deep subtropical moisture, produced widespread heavy precipitation across Utah. This led to flooding across many parts of the state, with significant damages reported in several locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A rope funnel cloud was spotted and photographed near Midway, Utah. The time listed is approximate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The moist airmass that was over Utah in late August continued through the first few days of September, helping produce several flash floods across the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was reported in the lower Escalante drainage, washing out some minor roads in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a brief break from showers and thunderstorms, significant moisture and instability returned to Utah, with flash flooding, large hail, and even a tornado reported across the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A debris flow was reported off of the Dump Fire burn scar, but prior mitigation was successful, as retention ponds filled up, but debris did not flow into the towns of Eagle Mountain or Saratoga Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a brief break from showers and thunderstorms, significant moisture and instability returned to Utah, with flash flooding, large hail, and even a tornado reported across the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds developed in Provo, with the Provo airport AWOS recording a peak wind gust of 59 mph. The most significant wind damage was a roof that was torn off of an apartment building. In addition, some smaller trees were knocked down, and hundreds of tree branches were downed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a brief break from showers and thunderstorms, significant moisture and instability returned to Utah, with flash flooding, large hail, and even a tornado reported across the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A small tornado was spotted and recorded by a member of the public. The tornado removed a metal roof from a barn, and did minor damage to trees and other outbuildings in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The moist airmass that was over Utah in late August continued through the first few days of September, helping produce several flash floods across the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large flash flood occurred along Deer Spring Wash and its drainages, including 4 feet of water coming out of some of the smaller washes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The moist airmass that was over Utah in late August continued through the first few days of September, helping produce several flash floods across the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Park rangers reported flooding in the Capitol Gorge section of Capitol Reef National Park, with water a foot high flowing through the Gorge, leading them to temporarily close the area to visitors. Additional flooding was also reported in and around other portions of Capitol Reef National Park, including impassable roads in Cathedral Valley and along the |Notom-Bullfrog Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The moist airmass that was over Utah in late August continued through the first few days of September, helping produce several flash floods across the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain over the Clay Springs Fire burn scar caused significant debris flows out of both the Fool Creek and Oak Creek drainages. However, prior mitigation efforts in Oak City protected homes from damage and flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The moist airmass that was over Utah in late August continued through the first few days of September, helping produce several flash floods across the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In the morning hours of September 2, heavy rain over State Route 6 caused a mudslide approximately 10 miles southeast of Soldier Summit. The road was closed for about 8 hours as crews cleared away the mud, and the slide temporarily closed parts of a construction project in the area. The mudslide also caused some damage to a local farm on the other side of the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a brief break from showers and thunderstorms, significant moisture and instability returned to Utah, with flash flooding, large hail, and even a tornado reported across the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A very large flash flood was reported in Wahweap Creek, Buckskin Gulch, and many other drainages in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The moist airmass that was over Utah in late August continued through the first few days of September, helping produce several flash floods across the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain just northeast of Panguitch, Utah caused flash flooding in the Sand Wash and Lime Kiln parts of town. Flooding in the agricultural area was significant, causing thousands of dollars of damage to fields and yards; however, no damage was reported to homes, and no injuries were reported to people or livestock.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The moist airmass that was over Utah in late August continued through the first few days of September, helping produce several flash floods across the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was reported in the town of Hildale, with some flood waters causing issues for four hours straight.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific storm system, combined with deep subtropical moisture, produced widespread heavy precipitation across Utah. This led to flooding across many parts of the state, with significant damages reported in several locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant long duration rainfall caused widespread flooding across Wayne County. State Route 24 was closed in several locations over the two day period, including between Caineville and Hanksville, as well as near Capitol Reef National Park, where the road was eroded due to the overflowing Fremont River. In addition, State Route 95 was damaged by flood waters and closed near Hanksville. Damage also occurred at Fish Creek Cove, where the road was cut off; this left about a dozen homes inaccessible, some of which were primary residences.||In Capitol Reef National Park, all of the streams and slot canyons were running high; the Scenic Drive was closed for an extended period of time, and many roads around the park sustained damage. Aside from roads, the most significant damage in Wayne County was at a diversion dam near Hanksville on the Fremont River, which will have to be completely rebuilt.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific storm system, combined with deep subtropical moisture, produced widespread heavy precipitation across Utah. This led to flooding across many parts of the state, with significant damages reported in several locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread flooding occurred across Canyonlands National Park, including the far western portions of the park. Many roads were closed due to flooding, and the entire park was even closed for a period of time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific storm system, combined with deep subtropical moisture, produced widespread heavy precipitation across Utah. This led to flooding across many parts of the state, with significant damages reported in several locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall fell over a wide area, resulting in flash flooding over most of the washes and drainages within the Grand Staircase-Escalante National Monument. There was also damage near the town of Escalante, as flood waters deposited rocks and mud on State Route 12, and some larger boulders also helped to damage the road.||Most local roads were closed for more than a day, and many of these roads sustained damage from flood waters, including significant damage to Hole-in-the-Rock Road. At least three culverts along Hole-in-the-Rock Road were damaged, and at least five culverts required extensive debris removal. By the end of September, road damage across all of Garfield County due to flooding was $320,000, most of which occurred in this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific storm system, combined with deep subtropical moisture, produced widespread heavy precipitation across Utah. This led to flooding across many parts of the state, with significant damages reported in several locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant flash flooding occurred in Herriman, South Jordan, and West Jordan. In Herriman, flood waters entered an apartment complex, impacting about 18 apartment units. In addition, minor flooding occurred at Herriman High School. In South Jordan, 11 homes in the Sunstone subdivision experienced basement flooding, with water several inches deep. In West Jordan, about 10 apartment units were flooded with water and mud, and portions of the Mountain View Corridor were closed so that the Utah Department of Transportation could drain retention ponds that had filled with the heavy rainfall.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sulphur Creek in Capitol Reef National Park experienced flash flooding following heavy rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific storm system moved into the Great Basin on September 24 and 25, producing strong prefrontal winds in Utah's northwest desert.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak wind gusts across the Dugway Proving Ground mesonet included 60 mph at the Causeway sensor, and 58 mph at both the North Salt Flats and Callao sensors.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific storm system, combined with deep subtropical moisture, produced widespread heavy precipitation across Utah. This led to flooding across many parts of the state, with significant damages reported in several locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant flooding impacted Emery County over two days, particularly on the west side of the county. Road damage and high water levels caused multiple highways to close down. State Route 10 closed from Castle Dale to Emery, with many boulders moving onto the road, some as large as cars. State Route 29 closed from mile post 0 to 12, or approximately Orangeville to Joe's Valley Reservoir. State Route 31 was closed multiple times through the event, in part due to multiple debris flows coming off of the Seeley Fire burn scar. In addition to the major routes listed here, many more local roads were closed due to flood waters, including Cottonwood Canyon. Monetary damages to roads were estimated to be approximately $150,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific storm system, combined with deep subtropical moisture, produced widespread heavy precipitation across Utah. This led to flooding across many parts of the state, with significant damages reported in several locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall fell over a wide area, resulting in widespread flash flooding. Similar to Garfield County, most of the washes and drainages within the Grand Staircase-Escalante National Monument experienced flooding, most local roads were closed for more than a day, and many of these roads sustained damage from flood waters, including significant damage to Hole-in-the-Rock Road.||In the central portion of Kane County, many drainages flooded, most notably along the Paria River. In addition, State Route 89 flooded from Kanab to Page.||On the far western part of Kane County, State Route 9 (also known as the Zion-Mt. Carmel Highway) was closed for an extended period of time at the east entrance of Zion National Park. Several sections of the road experienced flash flooding, including a ravine where flood waters blocked a road culvert and undercut the road. In total, road damage from this event in Kane County was approximately $185,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific storm system, combined with deep subtropical moisture, produced widespread heavy precipitation across Utah. This led to flooding across many parts of the state, with significant damages reported in several locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Long-duration heavy rainfall caused flooding in Washington County, particularly in Zion National Park and the town of Hurricane. Flooding in Zion National Park was widespread, impacting countless streams and washes. State Route 9 (also known as the Zion-Mt. Carmel Highway) was impacted on the Washington County side as well as in Kane County, and was closed all the way to the Canyon Junction, after debris and water damaged the road. ||In Hurricane, many roads across town experienced water and mud across the road, impairing traffic through the town. In addition, ten homes experienced some flooding; some had water flow into basements, while others simply had damage to lawns or garages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific storm system, combined with deep subtropical moisture, produced widespread heavy precipitation across Utah. This led to flooding across many parts of the state, with significant damages reported in several locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains caused flash flooding and a rockslide along State Route 143 between Parowan and Brian Head, with large boulders over the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific storm system, combined with deep subtropical moisture, produced widespread heavy precipitation across Utah. This led to flooding across many parts of the state, with significant damages reported in several locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain over multiple days resulting in flooding in Duchesne County, mostly impacting the town of Duchesne and State Route 191. Several streets in Duchesne were flooded, including Main Street, with the worst flooding occurring through the day of September 14. Earlier in the event, on the morning of September 12, multiple mudslides occurred on State Route 191 between Duchesne and Helper. The mud across the road trapped several motorists for a few hours before the debris could be cleaned up, but no injuries occurred due to the slides.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific storm system, combined with deep subtropical moisture, produced widespread heavy precipitation across Utah. This led to flooding across many parts of the state, with significant damages reported in several locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain over the Clay Springs Fire burn scar resulted in a debris flow along Fool Creek. Mud flowed out of the drainage, but did not cross the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific storm system, combined with deep subtropical moisture, produced widespread heavy precipitation across Utah. This led to flooding across many parts of the state, with significant damages reported in several locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding occurred along State Route 95, damaging portions of the road, especially near mile marker 31.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific storm system, combined with deep subtropical moisture, produced widespread heavy precipitation across Utah. This led to flooding across many parts of the state, with significant damages reported in several locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail with a diameter of one inch was reported in Hurricane, and a police car was damaged by the hailstones.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving storm system moved into the Great Basin on October 28. The main impact in Utah was strong, gusty winds, primarily in the western half of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong south winds developed across southwest Utah, with the strongest winds in Cedar City. The Cedar City ASOS recorded a peak wind gust of 71 mph, while the Milford ASOS recorded 64 mph. At least 15 large trees were knocked down by the winds; 10 of these trees were on the campus of Southern Utah University, one of which caused minor damage to a building. In addition, some roof damage was reported, including one barn that sustained significant roof damage from the strong winds.
