EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a brief break from showers and thunderstorms, significant moisture and instability returned to Utah, with flash flooding, large hail, and even a tornado reported across the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding and large waterfalls were reported throughout much of Zion National Park. In addition, the East Fork Virgin River Near Springdale, UT gauge rose from 34 cfs to 1400 cfs in one hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a brief break from showers and thunderstorms, significant moisture and instability returned to Utah, with flash flooding, large hail, and even a tornado reported across the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flash flooding across much of urban Utah County, with multiple locations recording 0.6-0.8 inches of rain in only 15 minutes. The most significant damage from flooding was reported in Provo, Orem, Payson, and Santaquin. Street flooding was widespread and some roads incurred major damage, with the worst damage occurring at 900 North and Grand Avenue in Provo, where water undercut and washed out a large portion of the asphalt road. Debris from the storm was scattered across most local roadways, taking several days to clean up, and even closed some roads for a period of time. Flowing water caused also caused significant damage to large concrete stairs at Lions Park in Provo. At University Mall in Orem, water quickly filled the parking lot, submerging many cars in more than a foot of water. ||Residential areas also received significant flood damage. In Provo, at least 25 homes had major damage, with more than 50 receiving minor damage. In Orem, approximately 30 homes received flood damage. In Santaquin, about 30 homes had flooding a foot deep or more, while about 30 other homes had flooding with less than a foot of water. Water wasn't the only problem in Santaquin, as the storm drainage system became overwhelmed, and raw sewage flowed into many homes. ||Finally, the flooding also impacted Timpanogos Cave National Monument, where heavy rain and associated debris stranded 150 hikers for several hours, before rangers were able to clear a path and rescue them. The Monument was closed for two full days while debris was cleaned up from both the hiking trails and nearby State Route 92.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving storm system moved into the Great Basin on October 28. The main impact in Utah was strong, gusty winds, primarily in the western half of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong southerly winds developed across the Great Salt Lake Desert, with the highest winds recorded in the Dugway Proving Ground mesonet. Peak wind gusts included 73 mph at Upper Cedar Mountain, 68 mph at Camel Back Mountain, 66 mph at Diddle Knoll, 62 mph at English Village, 60 mph at Gunnison Island, and 58 mph at Badger Island.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a brief break from showers and thunderstorms, significant moisture and instability returned to Utah, with flash flooding, large hail, and even a tornado reported across the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain over the Quail Hollow Fire burn scar caused a significant debris flow, with water and mud flowing down into the town of Alpine. About 100 residents were evacuated from their homes due to the imminent flooding. Roads across the area were inundated with mud, and 15 homes were damaged by the water and debris.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front pushed through the forecast area, followed by clearing skies which resulted in widespread freezing temperatures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minimum temperatures ranged from 18 to 30 degrees Fahrenheit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front pushed through the forecast area, followed by clearing skies which resulted in widespread freezing temperatures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minimum temperatures ranged from 19 to 29 degrees Fahrenheit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms continued across Utah for the beginning of August. These storms produced flash flooding across primarily central Utah, and one storm produced a strong microburst in the Salt Lake Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large debris flow occurred off of the Seeley Fire burn scar, covering State Route 31 with trees, mud, and rocks, as much as 1-2 feet deep in some locations. The debris flow damaged State Route 31, and closed the entire canyon for several hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a week where only isolated flash flooding impacted the state of Utah, a very moist air mass moved over the area once again. Flash flooding was reported in many locations across the state, including nearly-daily debris flows coming off the Seeley Fire burn scar.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On the northeast end of the Seeley Fire burn scar, flash flooding plugged culverts after one round of heavy rain, and then a larger flash flood occurred after a second thunderstorm moved over the area. In contrast to the flash flooding that typically flows over State Route 31, this water moved eastward toward Price.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms continued across Utah for the beginning of August. These storms produced flash flooding across primarily central Utah, and one storm produced a strong microburst in the Salt Lake Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large debris flow came off of the Wood Hollow Fire burn scar following heavy rainfall. The flow was generally 3 feet deep, and ran 4 miles out of the burn scar, but did not impact the town of Fountain Green.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms formed on August 12, producing flash flooding in Capitol Reef National Park and wind damage in northern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An isolated microburst over the Salt Lake Valley knocked over a large cottonwood tree at Wheeler Historic Farm. The time is approximate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoon moisture returned to Utah on August 17, helping to produce heavy thunderstorms across the state, which resulted in several flash floods.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused a flash flood on Huntington Creek and a debris flow off of the Seeley Fire burn scar. Flooding impacted the Huntington Power Plant, with water having to be diverted around the plant. Logs, mud, and rocks were in the river as it passed the power plant.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoon moisture returned to Utah on August 17, helping to produce heavy thunderstorms across the state, which resulted in several flash floods.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A debris flow occurred off of the Clay Springs Fire burn scar, with debris flowing out of the southwest aspect of the scar. Flood waters were as tall as four feet high as it ran toward US Route 50, but ran alongside the road rather than cross over it.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The deep monsoonal moisture remained over Utah through the end of August, resulting in more flash flooding and severe wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was reported on both Clear Creek and the East Fork of the Virgin River, as well as several slot canyons in the area. The river gauge on the East Fork rose to 1795 cfs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture remained in place over Utah, resulting in continued flash flooding in central and southern Utah, particularly at burn scars. In northern Utah, several thunderstorms became severe, producing gusty winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain over a large area resulted in the flash flooding of many drainages in far southern Utah, including the Paria River and Wahweap Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture remained in place over Utah, resulting in continued flash flooding in central and southern Utah, particularly at burn scars. In northern Utah, several thunderstorms became severe, producing gusty winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant and widespread flash flooding was reported across Zion National Park after more than an inch of rain fell over the park. The heavy rain filled culverts, and large rocks were washed onto the main scenic drive, resulting in the road being closed down for a period of time. Many canyons were running very high with water, and the gauge on the North Fork of the Virgin River rose to 3159 cfs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoon moisture returned to Utah on August 17, helping to produce heavy thunderstorms across the state, which resulted in several flash floods.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused a debris flow off the Seeley Fire burn scar, covering parts of State Route 32 with mud.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture remained in place over Utah, resulting in continued flash flooding in central and southern Utah, particularly at burn scars. In northern Utah, several thunderstorms became severe, producing gusty winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain over the Quail Fire burn scar near Alpine resulted in a debris flow and flash flooding. Mud flowed across several streets in Alpine, including Grove Drive, Box Elder Drive, and Moyle Drive. One home was flooded, with mud and water 18 inches deep flowing into the basement. One shopping center in the area also experienced some flooding as the water and mud flowed through town. Mud and debris took multiple days to clean up on many roads and lawns.||Associated with the same thunderstorm, flash flooding occurred along the Dry Creek Trail near American Fork Canyon, stranding two hikers for a couple hours before they could be rescued.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture remained in place over Utah, resulting in continued flash flooding in central and southern Utah, particularly at burn scars. In northern Utah, several thunderstorms became severe, producing gusty winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A debris flow occurred off of the Seeley Fire burn scar, covering State Route 31 with mud, and producing significant rises on Huntington Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture remained in place over Utah, resulting in continued flash flooding in central and southern Utah, particularly at burn scars. In northern Utah, several thunderstorms became severe, producing gusty winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain over the Wood Hollow Fire burn scar resulted in a debris flow, with mud crossing over State Route 132 between Fountain Green and Moroni.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Rockport Fire began due to a lightning strike on August 13, and caused significant damage in the town of Rockport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Rockport Fire began due to a lightning strike on August 13 near Wanship, Utah and Rockport State Park, and spread quickly into nearby residences. The fire grew to 1,920 acres before being effectively contained on August 18. ||Eight homes were destroyed by the fire, three within the Rockport Ranches area and five within the Rockport Estates area. The estimated value of these 8 homes was $550,000. In addition, 20 outbuildings were destroyed by the flames, and several vehicles and boats were damaged or destroyed. Some infrastructure was also damaged, with repairs needed to culverts and power lines.

CZ_NAME: GREAT SALT LAKE DESERT AND MOUNTAINS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Patch Springs Fire started near the Stansbury Mountains due to a lightning strike on August 10, and was contained two weeks later, but not before causing damage in the town of Terra.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Patch Springs Fire began due to a lightning strike on August 10, two miles from the community of Terra, Utah, which eventually received some damage as the fire spread. Property damage from this fire first occurred on August 17, and the fire eventually grew to 31,010 acres before being contained on August 24. ||The fire destroyed 5 trailer homes and significantly damaged 1 additional trailer home. In addition, 8 outbuildings and 1 commercial property were destroyed by the flames. Along Highway 199, 26 power poles were damaged or destroyed by the fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The deep monsoonal moisture remained over Utah through the end of August, resulting in more flash flooding and severe wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple sensors in the Wendover area recorded severe wind gusts from a thunderstorm that passed through the area, including peak gusts of 76 mph at the Wendover AWOS, 63 mph at the Interstate 80 at Milepost 1 sensor, and 59 mph at Wendover Peak.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The deep monsoonal moisture remained over Utah through the end of August, resulting in more flash flooding and severe wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding occurred in the Pine Valley area following a heavy thunderstorm. Five homes experienced basement flooding, with water as deep as 6 inches. Many roadways in the area had debris across the road, and a few roads sustained damage from the flood waters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The deep monsoonal moisture remained over Utah through the end of August, resulting in more flash flooding and severe wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding occurred in Croton Canyon, damaging one of the roads through that area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The deep monsoonal moisture remained over Utah through the end of August, resulting in more flash flooding and severe wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant flash flooding was reported along Chalk Creek following a heavy thunderstorm. The dramatically increased flow damaged two campgrounds in the area, washed out one bridge, and knocked down several trees along the banks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front pushed through the forecast area, followed by clearing skies which resulted in widespread freezing temperatures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minimum temperatures ranged from 21 to 32 degrees Fahrenheit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The final week of July saw even more flash flooding across Utah. The afternoons of July 26 and 27 were especially destructive, with record-breaking rainfall and widespread flooding. An isolated severe thunderstorm was also recorded during this period of time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant flash flooding occurred in Cedar City and Enoch, following record rainfall. The Cedar City ASOS recorded 2.25 inches of precipitation for the day, breaking the previous one-day record precipitation for the site. In Cedar City, 30 to 40 homes, as well as one church, experienced flooding, primarily in basements. In addition, heavy rain caused the roof of the Metalcraft Technologies building to collapse, though no one was in the building at the time. In Enoch, two homes and one public works building had significant flooding resulting in damage. Despite all this property damage, no injuries were reported due to the flood waters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The deep monsoonal moisture remained over Utah through the end of August, resulting in more flash flooding and severe wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large flash flood was reported in the Dry Valley drainage and its tributaries. This included along the Buckskin Wash, where the flood waters passed under State Route 89 around 6 pm MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a week where only isolated flash flooding impacted the state of Utah, a very moist air mass moved over the area once again. Flash flooding was reported in many locations across the state, including nearly-daily debris flows coming off the Seeley Fire burn scar.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding occurred in the town of Washington, with flooding beginning along Washington Drive and Interstate 15, and then spreading into the Coral Canyon subdivision. Two homes were especially impacted, with flood waters moving into their backyards. Using water pumps, the fire department was able to keep the flooding from entering one of the homes, but they were unable to keep waters from flowing into the second home. Water and mud as deep as two inches made it into the majority of the home, with interior damages estimated to be as high as $15,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms continued across Utah for the beginning of August. These storms produced flash flooding across primarily central Utah, and one storm produced a strong microburst in the Salt Lake Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large flash flood was reported in Capitol Reef National Park and near the town of Cainville. Water flowed across State Route 24 near Cainville, and several drainages in Capitol Reef National Park experienced flash flooding. Downstream, the Fremont River rose 4 feet from all the additional water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The final week of July saw even more flash flooding across Utah. The afternoons of July 26 and 27 were especially destructive, with record-breaking rainfall and widespread flooding. An isolated severe thunderstorm was also recorded during this period of time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread flash flooding occurred across Zion National Park. Canyons that were flooded included Heath, Pine, Spry, and Keyhole. The Emerald Pools were also flooded, and the Scenic Road had three areas where sand covered the road, requiring plows to remove the sand.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The weather pattern was favorable for another strong push of monsoon moisture northward into Utah and Colorado in mid September. The resultant thunderstorms produced heavy rainfall which contributed to flash flooding and debris flows across many areas on September 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was running through Armstrong and White canyons on the Natural Bridges National Monument.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The weather pattern of high pressure over the southeast states and an upper trough along the west coast allowed another surge of monsoonal moisture to move into eastern Utah. Cooling temperatures aloft allowed large hail to form in some of the stronger storms, while heavy rain and some flash flooding also occurred.
The persistent flow of monsoonal moisture resulted in strong thunderstorms which resulted in large hail and heavy rainfall in portions of southeast Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The majority of the hail that fell was around quarter size, with a few hail stones a bit larger.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The final week of July saw even more flash flooding across Utah. The afternoons of July 26 and 27 were especially destructive, with record-breaking rainfall and widespread flooding. An isolated severe thunderstorm was also recorded during this period of time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding occurred along the Fort Pearce Wash, with the gauge near St. George peaking at 4146 cfs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific storm system, combined with deep subtropical moisture, produced widespread heavy precipitation across Utah. This led to flooding across many parts of the state, with significant damages reported in several locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread flash flooding occurred once again across Wayne County due to heavy rainfall. State Route 24 was closed from Fruita to Hanksville, as flood waters flowed over the road. Capitol Reef National Park had flooding and debris along the Scenic Drive and the Grand Wash. Roads across the wide area again experienced damage due to the flood waters.
